name: Taj Mahal
clue: This ivory-white marble mausoleum was commissioned in 1632 by a Mughal emperor for his favorite wife.
fact: The Taj Mahal is located in Agra, India. It took about 20,000 artisans and 20 years to complete.

name: Mona Lisa
clue: This half-length portrait by Leonardo da Vinci is considered the most famous painting in the world.
fact: The Mona Lisa has been on permanent display at the Louvre in Paris since 1797. Her enigmatic smile has fascinated viewers for centuries.

name: Declaration of Independence
clue: This document, adopted on July 4, 1776, announced the thirteen American colonies' separation from Great Britain.
fact: The Declaration of Independence was primarily written by Thomas Jefferson. It asserts that 'all men are created equal' with unalienable rights.

name: Napoleon Bonaparte
clue: This French military leader rose to prominence during the French Revolution and became Emperor of France in 1804.
fact: Napoleon dominated European affairs for nearly two decades while leading France against a series of coalitions in the Napoleonic Wars.

name: Wright Brothers
clue: These two American siblings achieved the first powered, sustained, and controlled airplane flight in 1903.
fact: Orville and Wilbur Wright made the first flight on December 17, 1903 at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina. It lasted 12 seconds.

name: Eiffel Tower
clue: This wrought-iron lattice tower was built for the 1889 World's Fair and was the tallest structure in the world for 41 years.
fact: Designed by Gustave Eiffel's company, the tower stands 330 meters tall and was originally intended to be dismantled after 20 years.

name: Statue of Liberty
clue: This colossal neoclassical sculpture was a gift from France to the United States, dedicated in 1886.
fact: Designed by sculptor Frédéric Auguste Bartholdi, it stands on Liberty Island and has welcomed immigrants arriving from abroad for over a century.

name: Great Wall of China
clue: This series of fortifications stretches over 21,000 kilometers and was built to protect Chinese states from northern invasions.
fact: Construction began in the 7th century BC. The best-known sections were built by the Ming dynasty between 1368 and 1644.

name: Stonehenge
clue: This prehistoric monument of standing stones in Wiltshire, England was constructed roughly 5,000 years ago.
fact: Each of the larger sarsen stones weighs around 25 tons. How they were transported and erected remains partially a mystery.

name: Machu Picchu
clue: This 15th-century Inca citadel sits 2,430 meters above sea level on a mountain ridge in southern Peru.
fact: Built for the Inca emperor Pachacuti, Machu Picchu was abandoned during the Spanish conquest and rediscovered by Hiram Bingham in 1911.

name: Parthenon
clue: This former temple on the Athenian Acropolis was dedicated to the goddess Athena in the 5th century BC.
fact: The Parthenon is considered the most important surviving building of Classical Greece and a symbol of ancient Athenian democracy.

name: Colosseum
clue: This oval amphitheatre in Rome could hold 50,000 to 80,000 spectators and hosted gladiatorial contests and public spectacles.
fact: Construction began under emperor Vespasian around AD 72 and was completed in AD 80 under his son Titus.

name: Great Pyramid of Giza
clue: The oldest and largest of the three pyramids in the Giza complex, it was built as a tomb for Pharaoh Khufu around 2560 BC.
fact: It stood as the tallest man-made structure in the world for over 3,800 years, originally reaching 146.6 meters.

name: The Starry Night
clue: Painted in June 1889, this oil-on-canvas depicts a swirling night sky over a village, by a Dutch post-impressionist.
fact: Vincent van Gogh painted The Starry Night from the window of his room at the Saint-Paul-de-Mausole asylum in Saint-Rémy, France.

name: The Scream
clue: This iconic 1893 expressionist work by a Norwegian painter depicts an agonized figure against a blood-red sky.
fact: Edvard Munch created four versions of The Scream. He said the idea came to him during a sunset walk when he 'sensed an infinite scream passing through nature.'

name: Albert Einstein
clue: This German-born theoretical physicist developed the theory of relativity and won the 1921 Nobel Prize in Physics.
fact: Einstein's mass-energy equivalence formula E=mc² is one of the world's most famous equations. He emigrated to the U.S. in 1933.

name: Mahatma Gandhi
clue: This Indian lawyer led the nonviolent resistance movement that secured India's independence from British rule.
fact: Gandhi led the 1930 Salt March, a 240-mile protest against British salt taxes, becoming a symbol of peaceful resistance worldwide.

name: Abraham Lincoln
clue: This 16th U.S. President led the country through the Civil War and signed the Emancipation Proclamation in 1863.
fact: Lincoln was assassinated at Ford's Theatre by John Wilkes Booth on April 14, 1865, just days after the Civil War ended.

name: Winston Churchill
clue: This British Prime Minister led the United Kingdom to victory in the Second World War with his defiant wartime speeches.
fact: Churchill was also a prolific writer, winning the 1953 Nobel Prize in Literature for his historical works.

name: Julius Caesar
clue: This Roman general and statesman crossed the Rubicon in 49 BC, sparking civil war and becoming dictator of Rome.
fact: Caesar was assassinated on the Ides of March (March 15) in 44 BC by a group of senators led by Brutus and Cassius.

name: Leonardo da Vinci
clue: This Italian Renaissance polymath painted the Last Supper and filled thousands of notebook pages with inventions and studies.
fact: Leonardo designed flying machines, studied human anatomy through dissection, and engineered canals — centuries ahead of his time.

name: William Shakespeare
clue: This English playwright and poet wrote Hamlet, Macbeth, and Romeo and Juliet in the late 16th and early 17th centuries.
fact: Shakespeare wrote 39 plays and 154 sonnets. His works have been translated into every major living language.

name: Apollo 11 Moon Landing
clue: On July 20, 1969, humans walked on the lunar surface for the first time during this NASA mission.
fact: Neil Armstrong's first words on the Moon were 'That's one small step for man, one giant leap for mankind.' Buzz Aldrin followed 19 minutes later.

name: Fall of the Berlin Wall
clue: On November 9, 1989, East Germans were allowed to cross freely, leading to the dismantling of this Cold War barrier.
fact: The wall divided Berlin from 1961 to 1989. Its fall marked a turning point in the Cold War and led to German reunification in 1990.

name: RMS Titanic
clue: This British passenger liner sank in the North Atlantic in April 1912 after striking an iceberg on its maiden voyage.
fact: More than 1,500 of the 2,224 passengers and crew died. The wreck was discovered in 1985 about 3,800 meters below the surface.

name: Magna Carta
clue: This 1215 English charter limited the power of King John and became a foundational document of constitutional law.
fact: Sealed at Runnymede, Magna Carta established that the king was subject to the law. It influenced the U.S. Constitution centuries later.

name: Thomas Edison
clue: This American inventor held 1,093 U.S. patents and developed a practical incandescent light bulb and the phonograph.
fact: Edison founded the first industrial research laboratory in Menlo Park, New Jersey, pioneering the modern R&D system.

name: Marie Curie
clue: This Polish-French physicist and chemist pioneered research on radioactivity and was the first person to win Nobel Prizes in two sciences.
fact: Marie Curie discovered the elements polonium and radium. She won Nobel Prizes in Physics (1903) and Chemistry (1911).

name: Christopher Columbus
clue: This Italian explorer completed four voyages across the Atlantic Ocean starting in 1492, opening the way for European colonization of the Americas.
fact: Sponsored by the Catholic Monarchs of Spain, Columbus landed in the Bahamas on October 12, 1492, believing he had reached the East Indies.

name: Cleopatra
clue: This last active pharaoh of Ptolemaic Egypt famously aligned herself with Julius Caesar and later Mark Antony.
fact: Cleopatra VII ruled Egypt from 51 to 30 BC. Her death by suicide marked the end of the Ptolemaic Kingdom and its annexation by Rome.

name: Martin Luther King Jr.
clue: This American Baptist minister led the civil rights movement and delivered the 'I Have a Dream' speech in 1963.
fact: King won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1964 and was assassinated in Memphis, Tennessee on April 4, 1968.

name: Guernica
clue: This monumental 1937 anti-war painting by Pablo Picasso was created in response to the bombing of a Basque town during the Spanish Civil War.
fact: Guernica is roughly 25 feet wide and painted entirely in black, white, and gray. It hangs at the Museo Reina Sofía in Madrid.

name: Rosetta Stone
clue: This granodiorite stele inscribed with a decree in three scripts became the key to deciphering Egyptian hieroglyphs.
fact: Discovered in 1799 by French soldiers in Egypt, the stone dates to 196 BCE. French scholar Jean-François Champollion cracked its hieroglyphic script in 1822.

name: The Last Supper
clue: Leonardo da Vinci painted this late-15th-century mural of Jesus and the twelve apostles at their final meal, on a refectory wall of Santa Maria delle Grazie in Milan.
fact: Painted 1495–1498 using an experimental tempera-on-dry-plaster technique, the mural began deteriorating within decades and has undergone multiple major restorations.

name: The Creation of Adam
clue: This Michelangelo fresco on the Sistine Chapel ceiling shows God reaching out to give life to the first man, their fingertips nearly touching.
fact: Painted between 1508 and 1512 as part of the ceiling commissioned by Pope Julius II, the scene is the most reproduced religious image in Western art.

name: The Birth of Venus
clue: Sandro Botticelli painted this large Renaissance tempera of the goddess of love emerging fully grown from a scallop shell on the sea.
fact: Painted around 1485 for the Medici family in Florence, it hangs at the Uffizi Gallery alongside Botticelli's Primavera.

name: The Night Watch
clue: Rembrandt's 1642 group portrait shows a Dutch civic militia company marching into action, painted on a scale of nearly twelve by fourteen feet.
fact: Its full original title is 'Militia Company of District II under the Command of Captain Frans Banninck Cocq.' It hangs at the Rijksmuseum in Amsterdam.

name: Girl with a Pearl Earring
clue: Johannes Vermeer painted this small tronie of an anonymous girl in a blue-and-yellow turban, glancing over her shoulder with a luminous pearl in her ear.
fact: Painted around 1665, it has been called 'the Mona Lisa of the North' and is housed at the Mauritshuis museum in The Hague.

name: American Gothic
clue: Grant Wood's 1930 painting depicts a stern farmer holding a pitchfork beside a woman, with a white Gothic-windowed Iowa farmhouse behind them.
fact: The models were Wood's sister Nan and his dentist, not an actual farming couple. The work hangs at the Art Institute of Chicago.

name: The Great Wave off Kanagawa
clue: Katsushika Hokusai's ukiyo-e woodblock print shows a towering wave with claw-like foam about to crash over fishing boats, with Mount Fuji small in the distance.
fact: Part of his series Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji, the print was created around 1831 and went on to inspire Impressionist and modern Western artists.

name: Las Meninas
clue: Diego Velázquez painted this complex 1656 Spanish court scene centered on the Infanta Margarita, with the artist himself visible at his easel.
fact: A masterwork at the Museo del Prado in Madrid, it has been analyzed for centuries as a visual puzzle about perspective, power, and the act of painting.

name: The Garden of Earthly Delights
clue: Hieronymus Bosch's massive triptych moves from Eden through a surreal carnival of sin to a grotesque, fiery hell.
fact: Painted around 1490–1510 on oak panels, the work now hangs at the Prado in Madrid and remains one of art history's most enigmatic images.

name: Nighthawks
clue: Edward Hopper's 1942 painting shows three customers and a soda jerk inside a brightly lit late-night diner on a deserted American city street corner.
fact: Set in Greenwich Village, it is one of the most recognized images of American urban loneliness and hangs at the Art Institute of Chicago.

name: The Thinker
clue: Auguste Rodin sculpted this muscular seated male figure leaning forward with his chin on his hand in deep contemplation.
fact: Originally conceived in 1880 as part of Rodin's Gates of Hell, large-scale bronze casts now stand at more than two dozen sites around the world.

name: Venus de Milo
clue: This ancient Greek marble statue of Aphrodite, missing both arms, was discovered on the Aegean island of Milos in 1820.
fact: Carved around 150–125 BCE and attributed to Alexandros of Antioch, it is one of the most famous holdings of the Louvre in Paris.

name: David (Michelangelo)
clue: Michelangelo carved this 17-foot Renaissance marble nude depicting the biblical hero poised with a sling before his battle with Goliath.
fact: Completed in 1504 from a single block of Carrara marble, the statue is the centerpiece of the Galleria dell'Accademia in Florence.

name: Pieta (Michelangelo)
clue: Michelangelo sculpted this white Carrara marble of the Virgin Mary cradling the dead body of Christ, completed in 1499 when he was only in his mid-twenties.
fact: Housed in St. Peter's Basilica at the Vatican, it is the only work Michelangelo ever signed, after overhearing the sculpture attributed to another artist.

name: Campbell's Soup Cans
clue: Andy Warhol exhibited these 32 canvases in 1962, each painted in near-identical style to depict a single variety of the mass-market American soup.
fact: A founding work of American Pop Art, the set is now owned by the Museum of Modern Art in New York.

name: Battle of Waterloo
clue: This decisive 1815 battle in present-day Belgium ended the Napoleonic Wars when a coalition army defeated the French emperor for the final time.
fact: Fought on June 18, 1815, Waterloo pitted Napoleon's army against forces led by the Duke of Wellington and Prussian general Blücher and ended Napoleon's rule within days.

name: Black Death
clue: This mid-14th-century bubonic plague pandemic killed an estimated one-third of Europe's population and reshaped medieval society.
fact: Arriving in Europe via Genoese trading ships in 1347, the plague killed between 75 and 200 million people across Afro-Eurasia by 1353.

name: Chernobyl Disaster
clue: This 1986 nuclear accident in Soviet Ukraine became the worst civilian nuclear incident in history after a reactor exploded during a late-night safety test.
fact: Reactor No. 4 at the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant exploded on April 26, 1986, releasing radioactive fallout across Europe and forcing the permanent evacuation of the city of Pripyat.

name: Industrial Revolution
clue: This transformation of economies between roughly 1760 and 1840 replaced hand production with machines, beginning in Britain and spreading across Europe and North America.
fact: Innovations like the steam engine, power loom, and factory system drove mass urbanization, the rise of the working class, and modern capitalism.

name: Invention of the Printing Press
clue: Around 1440 in the German city of Mainz, this innovation paired movable metal type with an adapted wine press, making mass-produced books possible for the first time in Europe.
fact: Johannes Gutenberg's press produced the famous 42-line Bible around 1455 and set off a communications revolution that helped enable the Renaissance and Reformation.

name: September 11 Attacks
clue: On this day in 2001, al-Qaeda hijackers crashed four commercial airliners into the Twin Towers, the Pentagon, and a Pennsylvania field, killing nearly 3,000 people.
fact: The attacks led the United States to launch the War on Terror, invade Afghanistan, and establish the Department of Homeland Security.

name: Women's Suffrage (19th Amendment)
clue: Ratified in 1920, this amendment to the US Constitution prohibited federal and state governments from denying the right to vote on the basis of sex.
fact: The amendment was the culmination of a decades-long movement led by activists like Susan B. Anthony, Elizabeth Cady Stanton, and Alice Paul.

name: Code of Hammurabi
clue: This Babylonian stele from around 1754 BCE records one of the oldest surviving law codes, famous for its 'an eye for an eye' principle of retaliation.
fact: Inscribed in Akkadian cuneiform on a 7-foot basalt pillar by King Hammurabi of Babylon, the stele is now displayed at the Louvre in Paris.

name: Gettysburg Address
clue: Abraham Lincoln delivered this short 1863 speech at the dedication of a Union cemetery, recasting the American Civil War as a test of democracy.
fact: Lasting roughly two minutes and about 270 words, it has become one of the most quoted speeches in American history.

name: Gutenberg Bible
clue: This mid-15th-century printed edition of the Latin Vulgate Bible was the first major book produced in Europe using movable metal type.
fact: Johannes Gutenberg printed around 180 copies in Mainz around 1455. Roughly 49 are known to survive today, making them among the most valuable books in the world.

name: The Communist Manifesto
clue: This short 1848 political pamphlet by Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels argued that all history is a struggle between social classes and called for proletarian revolution.
fact: Commissioned by the Communist League and first published in London, the Manifesto opens with the famous line 'A spectre is haunting Europe—the spectre of communism.'

name: Treaty of Versailles
clue: Signed in 1919, this peace treaty formally ended World War I and imposed harsh territorial losses and reparations on Germany.
fact: Negotiated at the Paris Peace Conference and signed in the Hall of Mirrors at the Palace of Versailles, its punitive terms are often cited as a cause of World War II.

name: United States Bill of Rights
clue: Ratified in 1791, these first ten amendments to the US Constitution guarantee fundamental civil liberties such as free speech, free press, and the right to a fair trial.
fact: Drafted largely by James Madison, the Bill of Rights was added to address Anti-Federalist concerns that the original Constitution lacked explicit protections for individual freedoms.

name: Universal Declaration of Human Rights
clue: Adopted by the UN General Assembly in 1948, this post-war document set out fundamental rights belonging to every person regardless of nationality.
fact: Drafted by a committee chaired by Eleanor Roosevelt, the declaration has been translated into over 500 languages, more than any other document.

name: US Constitution
clue: Ratified in 1788, this founding document established the three branches of American federal government and replaced the weaker Articles of Confederation.
fact: Drafted in 1787 at the Philadelphia Convention and signed by 39 delegates including George Washington and Benjamin Franklin, it is the oldest written national constitution still in use.

name: Atlantic Charter
clue: This 1941 joint declaration by Franklin D. Roosevelt and Winston Churchill outlined Allied war aims and principles for a post-war world of self-determination and free trade.
fact: Signed aboard warships anchored off Newfoundland in August 1941, it laid intellectual groundwork for the United Nations and NATO.

name: Bayeux Tapestry
clue: This embroidered cloth nearly 70 meters long narrates the Norman conquest of England in more than fifty vivid scenes stitched in wool thread.
fact: Created in the 1070s, likely in England, it culminates in William the Conqueror's victory over King Harold at the Battle of Hastings in 1066 and is displayed today in Bayeux, France.

name: Dead Sea Scrolls
clue: This collection of ancient Jewish manuscripts was discovered between 1947 and 1956 in caves at Qumran near the Dead Sea.
fact: Dated roughly from the 3rd century BCE to the 1st century CE, the scrolls include the oldest known manuscripts of books later included in the Hebrew Bible.

name: Epic of Gilgamesh
clue: This Mesopotamian poem recounts the adventures of a Sumerian king and his wild companion Enkidu and is often called the oldest surviving great work of literature.
fact: Written in Akkadian cuneiform on clay tablets around 2100–1200 BCE, the epic includes a flood narrative strikingly similar to the later biblical story of Noah.

name: Twelve Tables
clue: Compiled around 451 BCE, this code of Roman law was the first written legal framework of the Roman Republic and formed the basis of later Roman jurisprudence.
fact: Originally inscribed on bronze tablets displayed in the Roman Forum, the Twelve Tables made Roman law publicly accessible and limited arbitrary rulings by patrician magistrates.

name: Galileo Galilei
clue: This Italian astronomer and physicist championed the heliocentric model, improved the telescope, and was famously tried by the Roman Inquisition in 1633.
fact: Galileo discovered Jupiter's four largest moons, the phases of Venus, and the rings of Saturn, earning him the nickname 'the father of modern science.'

name: Marco Polo
clue: This 13th-century Venetian merchant traveled the Silk Road to the court of Kublai Khan and described his journeys in a book that shaped European knowledge of Asia.
fact: His book, commonly known as 'The Travels of Marco Polo,' was dictated to a fellow prisoner in Genoa around 1298 and inspired later explorers, including Columbus.

name: D-Day (Normandy Landings)
clue: On June 6, 1944, Allied forces launched the largest seaborne invasion in history, landing on five beaches along the coast of Nazi-occupied Normandy, France.
fact: Codenamed Operation Neptune, the landings involved nearly 160,000 troops, over 5,000 ships, and more than 11,000 aircraft, and opened the Western Front in Europe.

name: Assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand
clue: The 1914 killing of the heir to the Austro-Hungarian throne in Sarajevo by a Bosnian Serb nationalist triggered the chain of events that led to World War I.
fact: Archduke Franz Ferdinand and his wife Sophie were shot by 19-year-old Gavrilo Princip on June 28, 1914. Within weeks, Europe's great powers had mobilized for war.

name: French Revolution
clue: This 1789–1799 upheaval overthrew the French monarchy, abolished feudal privileges, and produced the Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen.
fact: Sparked by the storming of the Bastille on July 14, 1789, the revolution led to the execution of King Louis XVI, the Reign of Terror, and eventually Napoleon's rise to power.

name: American Civil War
clue: This 1861–1865 conflict pitted the Union against eleven Southern states that had seceded as the Confederacy over disputes centered on slavery and states' rights.
fact: About 620,000 soldiers died, making it the deadliest war in American history. It ended with Confederate surrender at Appomattox and the abolition of slavery.

name: World War I
clue: This global conflict from 1914 to 1918 pitted the Allied Powers against the Central Powers and introduced industrial-scale trench warfare, tanks, and chemical weapons.
fact: Often called the Great War, it killed an estimated 20 million people and reshaped the map of Europe, ending the German, Austro-Hungarian, Russian, and Ottoman empires.

name: World War II
clue: This 1939–1945 global conflict pitted the Allies against the Axis powers and became the deadliest war in human history, ending with the defeat of Nazi Germany and Imperial Japan.
fact: An estimated 70–85 million people died. The war ended in Europe in May 1945 and in the Pacific in September 1945 after the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki.

name: Cuban Missile Crisis
clue: This tense 13-day 1962 standoff between the United States and Soviet Union over Soviet ballistic missiles in Cuba brought the world closer to nuclear war than any other Cold War event.
fact: The crisis ended on October 28, 1962 when Soviet leader Nikita Khrushchev agreed to remove the missiles in exchange for a US pledge not to invade Cuba and a secret withdrawal of US missiles from Turkey.

name: JFK Assassination
clue: On November 22, 1963, US President John F. Kennedy was shot and killed while riding in a presidential motorcade through Dealey Plaza in Dallas, Texas.
fact: Lee Harvey Oswald was arrested within hours but was himself shot two days later by nightclub owner Jack Ruby. The Warren Commission concluded Oswald acted alone.

name: Pearl Harbor Attack
clue: On December 7, 1941, the Imperial Japanese Navy launched a surprise aerial attack on the US Pacific Fleet anchored at this Hawaiian naval base, drawing America into World War II.
fact: The attack killed 2,403 Americans, damaged or sank 19 ships, and destroyed nearly 200 aircraft. The next day, President Roosevelt called it 'a date which will live in infamy.'

name: Hiroshima and Nagasaki Bombings
clue: In August 1945, the United States dropped atomic bombs on these two Japanese cities, the first and only use of nuclear weapons in armed conflict.
fact: 'Little Boy' fell on Hiroshima on August 6 and 'Fat Man' on Nagasaki on August 9. Combined, the bombings killed an estimated 200,000 people and led to Japan's surrender days later.

name: Fall of the Roman Empire
clue: The collapse of the Western Roman Empire is traditionally dated to 476 CE, when the Germanic chieftain Odoacer deposed the last emperor, Romulus Augustulus.
fact: Centuries of political instability, economic strain, and repeated barbarian invasions gradually weakened Rome. The Eastern, or Byzantine, Empire endured nearly 1,000 years longer.

name: Boston Tea Party
clue: On December 16, 1773, American colonists disguised as Mohawks boarded British ships in Boston Harbor and dumped 342 chests of tea into the water to protest British taxation.
fact: Organized by the Sons of Liberty, the protest escalated tensions with Britain and was a key event leading to the American Revolution.

name: Battle of Hastings
clue: On October 14, 1066, William the Conqueror's Norman forces defeated King Harold II's Anglo-Saxon army, permanently changing English history.
fact: King Harold died on the battlefield, reportedly shot in the eye with an arrow. The Norman conquest transformed English law, language, and ruling class, and is commemorated in the Bayeux Tapestry.

name: Discus Thrower (Discobolus)
clue: This iconic ancient Greek sculpture captures a male athlete at the peak moment of hurling a discus, his body coiled and balanced.
fact: Originally cast in bronze around 450 BCE by the Athenian sculptor Myron, the work is known today only through Roman marble copies that survived in museums like the British Museum and the Palazzo Massimo in Rome.

name: Republic
clue: USCIS civics question 1: What is the form of government of the United States?
fact: Accepted USCIS answers: Republic; Constitution-based federal republic; Representative democracy.

name: (U.S.) Constitution
clue: USCIS civics question 2: What is the supreme law of the land?
fact: Accepted USCIS answer: (U.S.) Constitution.

name: Forms the government
clue: USCIS civics question 3: Name one thing the U.S. Constitution does.
fact: Accepted USCIS answers: Forms the government; Defines powers of government; Defines the parts of government; Protects the rights of the people.

name: Self-government
clue: USCIS civics question 4: The U.S. Constitution starts with the words “We the People.” What does “We the People” mean?

wrong_options.name: The Vice President (of the United States)

fact: Accepted USCIS answers: Self-government; Popular sovereignty; Consent of the governed; People should govern themselves; (Example of) social contract.

name: Amendments
clue: USCIS civics question 5: How are changes made to the U.S. Constitution?
fact: Accepted USCIS answers: Amendments; The amendment process.

name: (The basic) rights of Americans
clue: USCIS civics question 6: What does the Bill of Rights protect?
fact: Accepted USCIS answers: (The basic) rights of Americans; (The basic) rights of people living in the United States.

name: Twenty-seven (27)
clue: USCIS civics question 7: How many amendments does the U.S. Constitution have?
fact: Accepted USCIS answer: Twenty-seven (27).

name: It says America is free from British control.
clue: USCIS civics question 8: Why is the Declaration of Independence important?
fact: Accepted USCIS answers: It says America is free from British control.; It says all people are created equal.; It identifies inherent rights.; It identifies individual freedoms..

name: Declaration of Independence
clue: USCIS civics question 9: What founding document said the American colonies were free from Britain?

wrong_options.name: To more closely follow public opinion

fact: Accepted USCIS answer: Declaration of Independence.

name: Equality
clue: USCIS civics question 10: Name two important ideas from the Declaration of Independence and the U.S. Constitution.
fact: Accepted USCIS answers: Equality; Liberty; Social contract; Natural rights; Limited government; Self-government.

name: Declaration of Independence
clue: USCIS civics question 11: The words “Life, Liberty, and the pursuit of Happiness” are in what founding document?

wrong_options.name: Advises the President (of the United States)

fact: Accepted USCIS answer: Declaration of Independence.

name: Capitalism
clue: USCIS civics question 12: What is the economic system of the United States?
fact: Accepted USCIS answers: Capitalism; Free market economy.

name: Everyone must follow the law.
clue: USCIS civics question 13: What is the rule of law?

wrong_options.name: It decides who is elected president.

fact: Accepted USCIS answers: Everyone must follow the law.; Leaders must obey the law.; Government must obey the law.; No one is above the law..

name: Declaration of Independence
clue: USCIS civics question 14: Many documents influenced the U.S. Constitution. Name one.
fact: Accepted USCIS answers: Declaration of Independence; Articles of Confederation; Federalist Papers; Anti-Federalist Papers; Virginia Declaration of Rights; Fundamental Orders of Connecticut; Mayflower Compact; Iroquois Great Law of Peace.

name: So one part does not become too powerful
clue: USCIS civics question 15: There are three branches of government. Why?
fact: Accepted USCIS answers: So one part does not become too powerful; Checks and balances; Separation of powers.

name: Legislative, executive, and judicial
clue: USCIS civics question 16: Name the three branches of government.
fact: Accepted USCIS answers: Legislative, executive, and judicial; Congress, president, and the courts.

name: Executive branch
clue: USCIS civics question 17: The President of the United States is in charge of which branch of government?
fact: Accepted USCIS answer: Executive branch.

name: (U.S.) Congress
clue: USCIS civics question 18: What part of the federal government writes laws?
fact: Accepted USCIS answers: (U.S.) Congress; (U.S. or national) legislature; Legislative branch.

name: Senate and House (of Representatives)
clue: USCIS civics question 19: What are the two parts of the U.S. Congress?
fact: Accepted USCIS answer: Senate and House (of Representatives).

name: Writes laws
clue: USCIS civics question 20: Name one power of the U.S. Congress.
fact: Accepted USCIS answers: Writes laws; Declares war; Makes the federal budget.

name: One hundred (100)
clue: USCIS civics question 21: How many U.S. senators are there?
fact: Accepted USCIS answer: One hundred (100).

name: Six (6) years
clue: USCIS civics question 22: How long is a term for a U.S. senator?
fact: Accepted USCIS answer: Six (6) years.

name: Answers will vary
clue: USCIS civics question 23: Who is one of your state’s U.S. senators now?

wrong_options.name: The Vice President (of the United States)

fact: Accepted USCIS answer: Answers will vary. District of Columbia residents and residents of U.S. territories should answer that D.C. or the territory where the applicant lives has no U.S. senators..

name: Four hundred thirty-five (435)
clue: USCIS civics question 24: How many voting members are in the House of Representatives?

wrong_options.name: (It states that the) powers not given to the federal government belong to the states or to the people.

fact: Accepted USCIS answer: Four hundred thirty-five (435).

name: Two (2) years
clue: USCIS civics question 25: How long is a term for a member of the House of Representatives?
fact: Accepted USCIS answer: Two (2) years.

name: To more closely follow public opinion
clue: USCIS civics question 26: Why do U.S. representatives serve shorter terms than U.S. senators?
fact: Accepted USCIS answer: To more closely follow public opinion.

name: Two (2)
clue: USCIS civics question 27: How many senators does each state have?
fact: Accepted USCIS answer: Two (2).

name: Equal representation (for small states)
clue: USCIS civics question 28: Why does each state have two senators?
fact: Accepted USCIS answers: Equal representation (for small states); The Great Compromise (Connecticut Compromise).

name: Answers will vary

fact: Accepted USCIS answer: Answers will vary. Residents of territories with nonvoting Delegates or Resident Commissioners may provide the name of that Delegate or Commissioner..

name: Mike Johnson

wrong_options.name: Advises the President (of the United States)

fact: Current answer: Mike Johnson. USCIS notes that this answer may change after elections or appointments; update this answer when the Speaker of the House changes.

name: Citizens of their state

wrong_options.name: Give up loyalty to other countries

fact: Accepted USCIS answers: Citizens of their state; People of their state.

fact: Accepted USCIS answer: Citizens from their state.

name: Citizens in their (congressional) district

fact: Accepted USCIS answers: Citizens in their (congressional) district; Citizens in their district; People from their (congressional) district; People in their district.

fact: Accepted USCIS answer: Citizens from their (congressional) district.

name: (Because of) the state’s population

fact: Accepted USCIS answers: (Because of) the state’s population; (Because) they have more people; (Because) some states have more people.

fact: Accepted USCIS answer: Four (4) years.

fact: Accepted USCIS answers: (Because of) the 22nd Amendment; To keep the president from becoming too powerful.

name: Donald Trump
clue: USCIS civics question 38: What is the name of the President of the United States now?
fact: Current answer: Donald Trump. USCIS notes that this answer may change after elections or appointments; update this answer when the President changes.

clue: USCIS civics question 39: What is the name of the Vice President of the United States now?
fact: Current answer: JD Vance. USCIS notes that this answer may change after elections or appointments; update this answer when the Vice President changes.

fact: Accepted USCIS answer: The Vice President (of the United States).

fact: Accepted USCIS answers: Signs bills into law; Vetoes bills; Enforces laws; Commander in Chief (of the military); Chief diplomat; Appoints federal judges.

name: The President (of the United States)

fact: Accepted USCIS answer: The President (of the United States).

name: The President (of the United States)

fact: Accepted USCIS answer: The President (of the United States).

fact: Accepted USCIS answer: The President (of the United States).

fact: Accepted USCIS answer: The President (of the United States).

fact: Accepted USCIS answers: President (of the United States); Cabinet; Federal departments and agencies.

fact: Accepted USCIS answer: Advises the President (of the United States).

fact: Accepted USCIS answers: Attorney General; Secretary of Agriculture; Secretary of Commerce; Secretary of Education; Secretary of Energy; Secretary of Health and Human Services; Secretary of Homeland Security; Secretary of Housing and Urban Development; Secretary of the Interior; Secretary of Labor; Secretary of State; Secretary of Transportation; Secretary of the Treasury; Secretary of Veterans Affairs; Secretary of War (Defense); Vice-President; Administrator of the Environmental Protection Agency; Administrator of the Small Business Administration; Director of the Central Intelligence Agency; Director of the Office of Management and Budget; Director of National Intelligence; United States Trade Representative.

wrong_options.name: Founded the first free public libraries

fact: Accepted USCIS answers: It decides who is elected president.; It provides a compromise between the popular election of the president and congressional selection..

fact: Accepted USCIS answers: Supreme Court; Federal Courts.

fact: Accepted USCIS answers: Reviews laws; Explains laws; Resolves disputes (disagreements) about the law; Decides if a law goes against the (U.S.) Constitution.

fact: Accepted USCIS answer: Supreme Court.

fact: Accepted USCIS answer: Five (5).

name: John Roberts

fact: Current answer: John Roberts. USCIS notes that this answer may change after appointments; update this answer when the Chief Justice changes.

name: Print paper money

fact: Accepted USCIS answers: Print paper money; Mint coins; Declare war; Create an army; Make treaties; Set foreign policy.

name: (It states that the) powers not given to the federal government belong to the states or to the people.

fact: Accepted USCIS answer: (It states that the) powers not given to the federal government belong to the states or to the people..

fact: Accepted USCIS answer: Answers will vary. District of Columbia residents should answer that D.C. is not a state and does not have a capital. Residents of U.S. territories should name the capital of the territory..

fact: Accepted USCIS answers: Citizens eighteen (18) and older (can vote).; You don’t have to pay (a poll tax) to vote.; Any citizen can vote. (Women and men can vote.); A male citizen of any race (can vote)..

fact: Accepted USCIS answers: Citizens; Citizens of the United States; U.S. citizens.

fact: Accepted USCIS answers: Give up loyalty to other countries; Defend the (U.S.) Constitution; Obey the laws of the United States; Serve in the military (if needed); Serve (help, do important work for) the nation (if needed); Be loyal to the United States.

fact: Accepted USCIS answers: Vote; Run for office; Join a political party; Help with a campaign; Join a civic group; Join a community group; Give an elected official your opinion (on an issue); Contact elected officials; Support or oppose an issue or policy; Write to a newspaper.

name: A holiday to honor soldiers who died in military service

fact: Accepted USCIS answer: A holiday to honor soldiers who died in military service.

name: A holiday to honor people in the (U.S.) military

name: Big Ben
clue: This famous clock tower rises above the Palace of Westminster and has become one of London’s most recognizable symbols.
fact: Big Ben is the nickname for the Great Bell inside Elizabeth Tower in London. The tower was completed in 1859.

name: Empire State Building
clue: This Art Deco skyscraper opened in Manhattan in 1931 and became an icon of the New York City skyline.
fact: The Empire State Building was the tallest building in the world when it opened and remains one of New York City’s most famous landmarks.

name: Christ the Redeemer
clue: This massive statue of Jesus stands atop Mount Corcovado overlooking Rio de Janeiro.
fact: Christ the Redeemer was completed in 1931 and is one of Brazil’s most recognizable monuments.

name: Motherland Calls
clue: This towering sword-bearing statue commemorates the Battle of Stalingrad in Volgograd, Russia.
fact: The Motherland Calls was unveiled in 1967 as the centerpiece of a memorial complex honoring the Battle of Stalingrad.

name: Berlin Wall
clue: This concrete barrier divided a major European capital from 1961 until its fall in 1989.
fact: The Berlin Wall divided East and West Berlin during the Cold War. Its fall in 1989 became a symbol of the end of the Cold War era.

name: Moai of Easter Island
clue: These monumental stone figures were carved by the Rapa Nui people on a remote Pacific island.
fact: The moai of Easter Island were carved between roughly the 13th and 16th centuries and are famous for their oversized heads and torsos.

name: Carnac Stones
clue: These prehistoric rows of standing stones stretch across Brittany in northwestern France.
fact: The Carnac Stones are one of the world’s largest collections of megalithic standing stones, with thousands of stones arranged in lines.

name: Chichen Itza
clue: This Maya city on Mexico’s Yucatán Peninsula is famous for the stepped pyramid known as El Castillo.
fact: Chichen Itza was a major Maya center and includes the Temple of Kukulcán, also known as El Castillo.

name: Tikal
clue: This ancient Maya city in Guatemala is known for its towering temples rising above the rainforest.
fact: Tikal was one of the most powerful cities of the ancient Maya civilization and is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

name: Pantheon
clue: This ancient Roman temple is famous for its massive dome and central oculus.
fact: The Pantheon in Rome was rebuilt under Emperor Hadrian and remains one of the best-preserved buildings from ancient Rome.

name: Temple of Luxor
clue: This ancient Egyptian temple complex stands on the east bank of the Nile in a city once known as Thebes.
fact: The Temple of Luxor was built over centuries by pharaohs including Amenhotep III and Ramesses II.

name: Pula Arena
clue: This remarkably preserved Roman amphitheater stands in modern-day Croatia.
fact: The Pula Arena is one of the best-preserved Roman amphitheaters and could hold thousands of spectators.

name: Theatre of Dionysus
clue: This ancient Greek theater on the slope of the Acropolis is associated with the birth of Greek drama.
fact: The Theatre of Dionysus in Athens hosted dramatic festivals and performances by playwrights such as Sophocles, Euripides, and Aristophanes.

name: Pyramid of the Sun
clue: This huge stepped pyramid dominates the ancient city of Teotihuacan near modern Mexico City.
fact: The Pyramid of the Sun is one of the largest pyramids in the world and was built by the civilization of Teotihuacan.

name: Step Pyramid of Djoser
clue: This early Egyptian pyramid at Saqqara was designed for Pharaoh Djoser by the architect Imhotep.
fact: The Step Pyramid of Djoser dates to Egypt’s Third Dynasty and is considered one of the earliest large stone monuments in history.
